settings.title: How to Read a Wine List Without the Anxiety
settings.excerpt: A few small habits make ordering far less stressful, and none of them involve memorizing regions.
settings.body:
People tell us all the time that wine lists make them nervous. The good news is that you do not need to know much to order well.

Tell us two words. Something like 'bright and light' or 'rich and savory' gives us more to work with than a grape name ever could.

Name a price by pointing. Point at a number on the list instead of saying it out loud. We will land near it, no awkwardness.

Trust the by-the-glass section. Those are the bottles we are most excited about right now, which is why they are open.

Why grape names are not the answer

It is easy to assume that knowing your grapes is the key, but in practice it tells us less than how you want the wine to feel. Two bottles of the same grape can taste worlds apart depending on where and how they were made. When you describe a sensation instead, we can match it across the whole list rather than steering you toward one familiar word. Nobody behind our bar will think less of you for it. We do exactly the same thing when we drink somewhere new.

If you want to sound a little more sure of yourself, a couple of small phrases do most of the heavy lifting. None of them require you to memorise a single region.

  • Ask what they are excited to pour tonight.
  • Say you want something that drinks easily without food.
  • Mention what you are eating and let the pairing follow.

The truth is that ordering wine is a conversation, not a test. Tell us roughly what you are after and trust us to fill in the rest, and you will end up with a glass you are glad you chose.
